soil_types: Well-Drained Sandy Loam Soil With Organic Matter

diseases.name: Powdery Mildew
diseases.impact: Initially appears as light yellow spots on mature leaves. Spots enlarge as white powdery mycelium grows on the leaf surface. Severely affected leaves turn brown, shrivel, and drop.
diseases.solution: Use recommended fungicides at the early stage of disease development. Remove infected crop residues after harvest.

diseases.name: Downy Mildew
diseases.impact: Angular yellow spots restricted by leaf veins on the upper surface of leaves. Leaves become chlorotic and eventually necrotic, leading to host cell death.
diseases.solution: Use recommended fungicides at the early stage of disease development. Remove infected plant parts after harvest.

diseases.name: Foot Rot
diseases.impact: In the early stages, plants show temporary wilting symptoms. Leaves turn yellow, wilt, and droop. Eventually, the plant dies. Vascular bundles in the basal region turn yellow or brown. White fungal mycelia can be seen at the base of the plant.
diseases.solution: Destroy infected plants with soil. Improve drainage. Use recommended fungicides at the early stage of disease development.

diseases.name: Gummy Stem Blight
diseases.impact: Lesions typically start in water-soaked areas on leaves and fruits. Early stages may show chlorotic holes, which then turn light brown and become irregular in shape. Dark, cracked, sunken spots appear on fruits. Sunken, cankerous lesions on the bark cause plants to break. Stem lesions often extend as streaks, and gum exudation may occur from cracks. Dark brown to black pycnidia can be seen on fruits, stems, or leaves.
diseases.solution: Plow crop residues deep immediately after harvest to reduce fungal survival. Use recommended fungicides at the early stage of disease development.

diseases.name: Bacterial Wilt
diseases.impact: Sudden wilting of leaves is observed. Eventually, the entire plant wilts. Leaves turn brown and dry. Vascular discoloration (browning) occurs. Permanent wilting.
diseases.solution: Destroy infected plants with soil. Crop rotation with resistant varieties (Okra, Maize). Intercropping with Cruciferae family crops. Avoid transferring equipment from infected fields to uninfected fields.

diseases.name: Leaf Curl Virus
diseases.impact: Upward curling of leaves, shortening of internodes, and distortion. Stunting of plants and deformation of fruits.

diseases.name: Papaya Ringspot Virus
diseases.impact: Mottled spots with variegation on leaves. Leaf deformities. Fruit deformities and reduced fruit size. In severe infections, oily spots appear on the fruit surface.

diseases.name: Cucumber Mosaic Virus
diseases.impact: Green mottling and leaf deformities. Green spots on fruits, turning bright yellow and decaying. Fruit deformities.
diseases.solution: No specific solution mentioned, but generally involves managing vectors and removing infected plants.

diseases.name: Zucchini Yellow Mosaic Virus
diseases.impact: Stunting of plants, yellowing, chlorosis (occasionally), variegated distorted leaves (blistering). Fruit discoloration and deformation.
diseases.solution: No specific solution mentioned, but generally involves managing vectors and removing infected plants.

diseases.name: Cucurbitaceae Phytoplasma Disease
diseases.impact: Small, thick, and yellowish-green leaves. Shortening of internodes and stunting of plants. Rosette formation. Greening of flowers.
diseases.solution: Remove infected plants. Control vectors (Leafhoppers) using recommended insecticides.
